goal: Plan and execute a transition plan for upcoming job change.
Coordinate handover for two major projects to minimize disruption during the transition.
Hit the ground running in the first 90 days and establish credibility with top three priorities (align on goals, deliver quick wins, establish reliable communications)
Develop and execute a structured handover/training plan for successor.
constraints: Follow security/HR policy on sharing information.
Maintain professional tone in resignation communications and avoid burning bridges.
Obtain consent to record training sessions and determine approved storage location.
Impact on relocation coverage and compensation terms when changing start date.
All compensation/benefit changes during delay must be documented in writing.
Tax treatment of relocation reimbursements must be confirmed with HR.

Acceptable reasons for delaying start date beyond logistics include: completing a critical obligation at current job, family or caregiving responsibilities, health/medical needs, visa/work-authorization timing, housing constraints, child/school timing, required certifications or training, security clearances or background investigations, military service or legally required leave, and prior contractual commitments.

Guidance on presenting a late-start request: be specific and time-bounded, offer practical alternatives (remote onboarding, participation in key meetings, documented handoff), and obtain written confirmation of any changes to compensation or relocation terms.
decisions: Decide target notice length: 2 weeks vs 3–4 weeks (overlap considerations)
Pursue written confirmation of flexibility terms for the first months from manager or HR
Clarify PTO accrual cash-out policy during notice period
Decide relocation funding method with employer (direct vendor billing vs reimbursement)
